UNICEF Data Story: Postnatal Care and Life Expectancy

Author

(BAA1030)

Context and purpose

You are a data analyst at UNICEF. The goal of this report is to raise awareness of the public on a specific world issue using the provided datasets and a narrative supported by visualisations.

This report is an HTML report rendered by Quarto and designed for GitHub Pages publication. It includes four required plotnine visualisations: a world map, a bar chart, a scatterplot with a linear regression line, and a time-series chart.

World issue

Unequal access to postnatal care in the first 48 hours after birth, and how this relates to life expectancy as a broad measure of population health.

Headline facts (from the data)

  • Postnatal care coverage (latest year per country) is available for 112 countries.
  • Latest postnatal care values range from 5.0% to 99.8%.
  • When we align the latest postnatal care year with metadata, 108 countries also have life expectancy in the same year.
  • In that aligned set, life expectancy ranges from 31.5 to 80.1 years.

Story (narrative)

Why postnatal care matters

The first two days after birth are critical. Postnatal care within 48 hours can identify complications, support recovery, and connect mothers to follow-up services. If access is limited, preventable complications can go unnoticed.

What we expect to see

If a country has stronger health system access overall, we might expect both higher postnatal care coverage and higher life expectancy. The charts below show where postnatal care is highest and lowest globally, and then examine its relationship with life expectancy.

Visualisation 1 (required) - World map: latest postnatal care (%)

Visualisation 2 (required) - Bar chart: lowest postnatal care countries

Visualisation 3 (required) - Scatterplot with regression: postnatal care vs life expectancy

Visualisation 4 (required) - Time-series: postnatal care over time

Call to action

Improving postnatal care within the first 48 hours after birth is one of the most practical ways to strengthen maternal health systems. The pattern in this report suggests that countries with higher postnatal care coverage also tend to achieve better life expectancy outcomes. Policymakers and partners should prioritise frontline services: staffing for immediate post-delivery follow-up, removing cost and travel barriers, and integrating postnatal check-ups into routine maternal care. Targeting the lowest-coverage countries identified here is a clear opportunity to improve health outcomes at scale.

Limitations and next steps

  • National averages can hide sub-national inequalities.
  • The charts show association, not causation.
  • The life expectancy join uses the same year as each country’s latest postnatal care observation; countries missing that metadata-year are excluded from the scatterplot.

## Context and purpose

You are a data analyst at **UNICEF**. The goal of this report is to raise awareness of the public on a specific world issue using the provided datasets and **a narrative supported by visualisations**.

This report is an **HTML report rendered by Quarto** and designed for **GitHub Pages** publication. It includes **four required plotnine visualisations**: a world map, a bar chart, a scatterplot with a linear regression line, and a time-series chart.

### World issue

**Unequal access to postnatal care** in the first 48 hours after birth, and how this relates to **life expectancy** as a broad measure of population health.

---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

import os
import urllib.request

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd

# Required plotting library
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes, geom_col, geom_point, geom_smooth, geom_line, geom_polygon,
    scale_fill_cmap, labs, theme_minimal, theme, element_text, coord_flip
)

# For the world map polygon base layer
import geopandas as gpd
from shapely.geometry import Polygon, MultiPolygon


def load_csv(path: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Load a CSV with defensive parsing (polars)."""
    return pl.read_csv(path, ignore_errors=True, null_values=['', 'NA', 'NaN'])


def standardise_indicator(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Ensure expected columns exist and cast types where safe."""
    expected = ['country','alpha_3_code','time_period','obs_value','indicator']
    missing = [c for c in expected if c not in df.columns]
    if missing:
        raise ValueError(f"Missing expected columns: {missing}")

    return (
        df
        .with_columns([
            pl.col('time_period').cast(pl.Int64, strict=False),
            pl.col('obs_value').cast(pl.Float64, strict=False),
            pl.col('alpha_3_code').cast(pl.Utf8, strict=False),
            pl.col('country').cast(pl.Utf8, strict=False),
            pl.col('indicator').cast(pl.Utf8, strict=False)
        ])
        .filter(pl.col('alpha_3_code').is_not_null())
        .filter(pl.col('time_period').is_not_null())
        .filter(pl.col('obs_value').is_not_null())
    )


def latest_by_country(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Pick the latest time_period per ISO3 country."""
    return (
        df
        .sort(['alpha_3_code','time_period'])
        .group_by('alpha_3_code')
        .agg([
            pl.last('country').alias('country'),
            pl.last('time_period').alias('latest_year'),
            pl.last('obs_value').alias('latest_value'),
            pl.last('indicator').alias('indicator')
        ])
    )


def world_polygons() -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Long-form polygon DataFrame for plotnine (geom_polygon).

    GeoPandas 1.0 removed built-in sample datasets, so we download Natural Earth
    countries (110m) as a zip (once) and read it locally.
    """
    url = "https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
    local_zip = "data/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip"
    os.makedirs("data", exist_ok=True)

    if not os.path.exists(local_zip):
        urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, local_zip)

    world = gpd.read_file(local_zip)

    # Natural Earth typically uses ISO_A3; normalise to iso_a3
    if "ISO_A3" in world.columns and "iso_a3" not in world.columns:
        world["iso_a3"] = world["ISO_A3"]

    world = world[world["iso_a3"].notna()].copy()

    rows = []
    group_id = 0

    def add_polygon(poly, iso_a3: str):
        nonlocal group_id
        x, y = poly.exterior.coords.xy
        rows.extend(
            {"iso_a3": iso_a3, "long": float(xx), "lat": float(yy), "group": group_id}
            for xx, yy in zip(x, y)
        )
        group_id += 1

    for iso, geom in zip(world["iso_a3"], world.geometry):
        if geom is None:
            continue
        if geom.geom_type == "Polygon":
            add_polygon(geom, iso)
        elif geom.geom_type == "MultiPolygon":
            for poly in geom.geoms:
                add_polygon(poly, iso)

    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


# Paths (ONLY indicator 1 + metadata)
CARE_PATH = 'data/unicef_indicator_1.csv'
META_PATH = 'data/unicef_metadata.csv'

care_raw = load_csv(CARE_PATH)
meta_raw = load_csv(META_PATH)

care = standardise_indicator(care_raw)

# Metadata: align indicator years to metadata 'year'
meta = (
    meta_raw
    .with_columns([
        pl.col('year').cast(pl.Int64, strict=False),
        pl.col('alpha_3_code').cast(pl.Utf8, strict=False),
        pl.col('Life expectancy at birth, total (years)').cast(pl.Float64, strict=False)
    ])
)

care_latest = latest_by_country(care)

# Join to life expectancy in the SAME year as the latest postnatal care observation
care_for_join = care_latest.rename({'latest_year':'year', 'latest_value':'postnatal_care_pct'})

joined = (
    care_for_join
    .join(
        meta.select(['alpha_3_code','year','Life expectancy at birth, total (years)']),
        on=['alpha_3_code','year'],
        how='left'
    )
    .rename({'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)':'life_expectancy_years'})
    .filter(pl.col('life_expectancy_years').is_not_null())
)

# Headline stats for narrative (computed during render)

n_care_countries = care_latest.height
care_min = care_latest.select(pl.min('latest_value')).item()
care_max = care_latest.select(pl.max('latest_value')).item()

n_joined = joined.height
le_min = joined.select(pl.min('life_expectancy_years')).item()
le_max = joined.select(pl.max('life_expectancy_years')).item()

# Bar chart dataset
bottom15 = care_latest.sort('latest_value').head(15)

# Time-series selection: countries with longest series
series_counts = (
    care
    .group_by(['alpha_3_code','country'])
    .agg(pl.len().alias('n_obs'))
    .sort('n_obs', descending=True)
    .head(6)
)
sel_codes = series_counts['alpha_3_code'].to_list()

care_ts = (
    care
    .filter(pl.col('alpha_3_code').is_in(sel_codes))
    .select(['country','time_period','obs_value'])
    .rename({'time_period':'year','obs_value':'postnatal_care_pct'})
    .sort(['country','year'])
)
```

## Headline facts (from the data)

```{python}
#| label: headline-facts
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

from IPython.display import Markdown, display

display(Markdown(f"""
- Postnatal care coverage (latest year per country) is available for **{n_care_countries}** countries.  
- Latest postnatal care values range from **{care_min:.1f}%** to **{care_max:.1f}%**.  
- When we align the latest postnatal care year with metadata, **{n_joined}** countries also have life expectancy in the same year.  
- In that aligned set, life expectancy ranges from **{le_min:.1f}** to **{le_max:.1f}** years.
"""))
```

## Story (narrative)

### Why postnatal care matters

The first two days after birth are critical. Postnatal care within 48 hours can identify complications, support recovery, and connect mothers to follow-up services. If access is limited, preventable complications can go unnoticed.

### What we expect to see

If a country has stronger health system access overall, we might expect both higher postnatal care coverage and higher life expectancy. The charts below show where postnatal care is highest and lowest globally, and then examine its relationship with life expectancy.

---

## Visualisation 1 (required) - World map: latest postnatal care (%)

```{python}
#| label: fig-world-map
#| fig-cap: "World map of the latest available postnatal care (%) by country (ISO3 join)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

poly = world_polygons()
care_latest_pd = care_latest.to_pandas().rename(columns={'alpha_3_code':'iso_a3', 'latest_value':'postnatal_care_pct'})
map_df = poly.merge(care_latest_pd[['iso_a3','postnatal_care_pct']], on='iso_a3', how='left')

(
    ggplot(map_df, aes('long', 'lat', group='group', fill='postnatal_care_pct'))
    + geom_polygon(color='white', size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_cmap(name='Postnatal care (%)', cmap_name='viridis')
    + labs(
        title='Postnatal care within 2 days of birth (latest available)',
        subtitle='Countries without data appear in grey',
        x='', y=''
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(11, 5.5), plot_title=element_text(weight='bold'))
)
```

## Visualisation 2 (required) - Bar chart: lowest postnatal care countries

```{python}
#| label: fig-bar-bottom
#| fig-cap: "Bottom 15 countries by latest postnatal care (%)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

bottom15_pd = bottom15.with_columns(pl.col('latest_value').round(1)).to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(bottom15_pd, aes('reorder(country, latest_value)', 'latest_value'))
    + geom_col(fill='#2c7fb8')
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title='Lowest postnatal care coverage (latest year per country)',
        x='Country',
        y='Postnatal care (%)'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 5), plot_title=element_text(weight='bold'))
)
```

## Visualisation 3 (required) - Scatterplot with regression: postnatal care vs life expectancy

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter-reg
#| fig-cap: "Postnatal care (%) vs life expectancy (years), with a linear regression line (same-year alignment)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

scatter = joined.select(['country','postnatal_care_pct','life_expectancy_years']).to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(scatter, aes('postnatal_care_pct', 'life_expectancy_years'))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.65, color='#f03b20')
    + geom_smooth(method='lm', se=True, color='black')
    + labs(
        title='Postnatal care and life expectancy move together in many countries',
        subtitle='Life expectancy is taken from metadata in the same year as the latest postnatal care value',
        x='Postnatal care within 2 days (%)',
        y='Life expectancy at birth (years)'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 5), plot_title=element_text(weight='bold'))
)
```

## Visualisation 4 (required) - Time-series: postnatal care over time

```{python}
#| label: fig-time-series
#| fig-cap: "Time-series of postnatal care (%) for selected countries (chosen by longest available series)."
#| echo: false
#| warning: false

care_ts_pd = care_ts.to_pandas()

(
    ggplot(care_ts_pd, aes('year', 'postnatal_care_pct', color='country'))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + labs(
        title='Postnatal care trends over time (selected countries)',
        subtitle='Countries selected programmatically by longest available time-series',
        x='Year',
        y='Postnatal care within 2 days (%)',
        color='Country'
    )
    + theme_minimal()
    + theme(figure_size=(10, 5), plot_title=element_text(weight='bold'))
)
```

## Call to action

Improving postnatal care within the first 48 hours after birth is one of the most practical ways to strengthen maternal health systems. The pattern in this report suggests that countries with higher postnatal care coverage also tend to achieve better life expectancy outcomes. Policymakers and partners should prioritise frontline services: staffing for immediate post-delivery follow-up, removing cost and travel barriers, and integrating postnatal check-ups into routine maternal care. Targeting the lowest-coverage countries identified here is a clear opportunity to improve health outcomes at scale.

## Limitations and next steps

- National averages can hide sub-national inequalities.
- The charts show association, not causation.
- The life expectancy join uses the same year as each country's latest postnatal care observation; countries missing that metadata-year are excluded from the scatterplot.
